title: SSC CGL T-I English Section Test

questions.question: Choose the option that completes the sentence using the correct spelling. The ______ silence in the room made everyone uneasy.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (a). Option (A) is the correctly spelt word. Correct spelling is: “ominous”. It means giving the impression that something bad or unpleasant is going to happen; threatening or menacing in nature (Hindi meaning: अशुभ संकेत देने वाला / अनिष्टसूचक). It is commonly used for situations, signs, or feelings that create fear or anxiety about the future. Example: The ominous silence before the announcement worried everyone in the hall. Meanings of all the given options: • Option A: Ominous – suggesting future trouble or danger (अशुभ/अनिष्टसूचक). • Option B: Ominus – not a standard English spelling; incorrect form (गलत वर्तनी). • Option C: Omineous – incorrect spelling; not acceptable in standard English (गलत वर्तनी). • Option D: Omminous – incorrect spelling due to double ‘m’; non-standard form (गलत वर्तनी). So the correct answer is (a)

questions.question: Choose the correct meaning of idiom "Hoist with one's own petard".
options: Be harmed by one’s own plot
questions.explanation: The correct option is (b). Meaning of the idiom: Hoist with one's own petard means to be harmed or defeated by one’s own plan, trick, or plot intended for others. A “petard” was a small explosive; the idiom suggests the plotter is blown up by his own device. (Hindi meaning/idiomatic sense: अपने ही जाल में फँस जाना / अपनी ही चाल से नुकसान होना) Example: He spread false rumours, but the truth came out and he was hoist with his own petard. Why other options are incorrect: • (a) relates to flattery, which is not the idea here. • (c) refers to success through effort, which is unrelated. • (d) means escaping danger, which is opposite to being harmed by one’s own plan. Other related idioms and meanings: • Dig one’s own grave: create one’s own downfall (अपना ही नुकसान करना). • Backfire: produce the opposite effect intended (उल्टा असर होना). • Catch-22: a situation with no good solution due to contradictory rules (दुविधापूर्ण स्थिति). So the correct answer is (b)

questions.question: Choose the correct indirect speech form of the following sentence. Ritu said to Neha, “What will you buy for me on my birthday next year?”
options: Ritu asked Neha what she would buy for her on her birthday the following year.
Ritu asked Neha what she would buy for her on her birthday the previous year.
Ritu asked Neha what she should buy for her on her birthday next year.
Ritu requested Neha what she should buy for her on her birthday the coming year.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (a). To change direct to indirect speech for a question, we use the reporting verb “asked” and remove the question form by using statement order (subwect + verb). We also apply tense backshift when the reporting verb is in the past (“said”), and we change pronouns and time expressions appropriately. Step-by-step rules applied: • Reporting verb change: “said to” becomes “asked” because it is an interrogative sentence. • Remove quotation marks and question mark; use indirect question form. • Change word order: “What will you buy” becomes “what she would buy” (no auxiliary inversion in indirect questions). • Tense backshift: “will” changes to “would” because the reporting verb is in the past. • Pronoun changes: “you” (addressing Neha) becomes “she”; “me” (Ritu) becomes “her”. • Time expression change: “next year” becomes “the following year” in indirect speech. • Possessive/time reference: “on my birthday” becomes “on her birthday” referring to Ritu. Therefore, option (A) correctly follows all conversion rules. So the correct answer is (a)

questions.question: Prior to the digitisation of the data, how was the solar imagery data stored?
options: It was stored using a high-resolution digital format
It was stored using photographic plates and films
It was stored using a high-resolution analogue format
questions.explanation: Correct answer: (c) It was stored using photographic plates and films Explanation: The passage clearly states that earlier the data was not digital. It says: “The data was historically archived in photographic plates and film. After digitisation, the images are preserved in high-resolution digital format.” • Hence, before digitisation, the solar imagery was stored in photographic plates and film. • Options (a) and (b) refer to the post-digitisation method (high-resolution digital format and FITS). • Option (d) is incorrect because the passage specifically names photographic plates and film, not “high-resolution analogue format.” Why other options are incorrect (proper explanation): • (a) High-resolution digital format = mentioned after digitisation, not before. • (b) FITS = the digital file format used after digitisation. • (d) High-resolution analogue format = vague and not stated; the passage gives exact storage: plates and film.

questions.question: Where is the Kodaikanal Solar Observatory situated?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: (b) Tamil Nadu Explanation: The opening line of the passage states: “staff at the Kodaikanal Solar Observatory in Tamil Nadu…” • Therefore, the observatory is located in Tamil Nadu. Why other options are incorrect (proper explanation): • (a) Karnataka = not mentioned; incorrect state. • (c) Andhra Pradesh = not mentioned; incorrect state. • (d) Telangana = not mentioned; incorrect state.

questions.question: According to the passage, which of the following statements is incorrect about the data collected from Kodaikanal Solar Observatory?
options: It can be used for academic purposes to understand the long-term behaviour of the sun
It helps understand sunspot activity that impacts climate and telecommunication systems
It provides an understanding of mawor events affecting the earth’s magnetic field
According to Dipankar Banerwee, the data helps only in studying past sunspot activity
questions.explanation: Correct answer: (d) According to Dipankar Banerwee, the data helps only in studying past sunspot activity Explanation: The passage quotes Dipankar Banerwee saying that the knowledge from this data helps understand current and future events and also helps predict future sunspot activity levels with better accuracy. • Statement (d) says “only” studying past sunspot activity, which directly contradicts the passage. • The passage clearly mentions future prediction, so “only past” is incorrect. Why other options are correct (so not the answer): • (a) Correct: the passage says it is used in academic studies of long-term behaviour of the sun. • (b) Correct: the passage says it helps understand sunspot activity impacting climate and telecommunication systems. • (c) Correct: the passage says it throws light on mawor events that impacted earth’s magnetic field.

questions.question: What is the reason for taking three different images of the sun?
options: Because features called filaments can be viewed
Because different filters create an overall better image
Because the data spans over a hundred years
Because the sun has a layered structure and each filter exposes a different layer
questions.explanation: Correct answer: (d) Because the sun has a layered structure and each filter exposes a different layer Explanation: The passage states: “It is known that the sun has a layered structure, and each of these data sets exposes a different layer.” • White light shows photosphere and sunspots. • Calcium-K shows layers ~2,000 km above, in the chromosphere. • H-alpha shows layers a little above Calcium-K. • Therefore, three images are taken to capture different layers of the sun. Why other options are incorrect (proper explanation): • (a) Filaments can be viewed, but this is a result/feature, not the main reason. The main reason is the layered structure. • (b) “overall better image” is not stated; the reason is layer-wise exposure. • (c) Spanning hundred years is a special quality of the data, not the reason for three filters.

questions.question: According to the passage, which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: The Kodaikanal Solar Observatory has been functioning since 1902 or earlier
Each image of the sun is taken using all three filters simultaneously
The white light filter exposes the photosphere where sunspots are visible
Filaments are related to large expulsions of material from the sun’s surface
questions.explanation: Correct answer: (b) Each image of the sun is taken using all three filters simultaneously Explanation: The passage says there are three sets of images, taken using different filters (White light, H-alpha, Calcium-K). It does not say they are taken simultaneously. • The statement (b) adds an extra idea “simultaneously” which is not supported by the passage, so it is incorrect. Why other options are correct (so not the answer): • (a) Correct: the passage says spectroheliograms were taken since 1902, and daily aimed since 1904, so functioning since 1902 or earlier is supported. • (c) Correct: the passage clearly states white light shows photosphere and sunspots. • (d) Correct: the passage says filaments are related to large expulsions of material from the sun’s surface.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate synonym of the given word. Salutary
questions.explanation: The correct option is (c) Beneficial. Salutary means producing a good effect; beneficial or health-giving. It is used for actions, advice, or changes that lead to improvement or well-being (हितकर/कल्याणकारी). Example: Regular exercise has a salutary effect on both body and mind. Beneficial means helpful or advantageous; something that improves a condition or results in good outcomes (लाभदायक/फायदेमंद). Example: Drinking enough water is beneficial for maintaining good health. Synonyms: advantageous, helpful, wholesome, healthful. Antonyms: harmful, detrimental, inwurious, adverse. Meanings of all the other given options: • A: Adversarial means hostile or opposing (विरोधी/शत्रुतापूर्ण). • B: Lively means full of life and energy (चुस्त/उत्साही). • D: Solely means only; exclusively (केवल/सिर्फ). So the correct answer is (c)

questions.question: Select the word that is opposite in meaning (ANTONYM) to the word given below. Pagan
questions.explanation: Correct answer: (a) Religious Explanation: “Pagan” refers to a person who follows a non-Christian/non-monotheistic religion, or in exam context, non-religious/heathen (not following the main established religion). • The opposite in common exam usage is Religious (faithful/devout). Word Meaning (English): Pagan = heathen; non-Christian; not following a mawor religion Hindi Meaning: Pagan = मूर्तिपूजक/गैर-धार्मिक (परीक्षा-उपयोग में: अधार्मिक/हिथेन) Example (Pagan): Many ancient pagan rituals were linked to nature worship. Meaning of the correct option: • Religious = devout; following religion Hindi Meaning: Religious = धार्मिक/आस्थावान Example (Religious): He comes from a very religious family. Synonyms (Pagan): heathen, non-believer, idolater (context-based) Antonyms (Pagan): religious, devout, godly Meanings of other options (why incorrect): • (b) Sacrilegious = disrespectful to religion (धर्म का अपमान करने वाला) → not exact opposite; it is negative but still religion-related. • (c) Courageous = बहादुर → unrelated. • (d) Matins = morning prayers (प्रातःकालीन प्रार्थना) → noun, not an antonym pair for “pagan”.

questions.question: Change the following from active to passive: They have introduced several measures to improve classroom participation.
options: Several measures have been introduced to improve classroom participation.
Several measures were introduced by them to improve classroom participation.
Several measures are being introduced by them to improve classroom participation.
Several measures are introduced by them for improving classroom participation.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (a). Step-by-step conversion (Active to Passive): • Identify the tense: “have introduced” is Present Perfect (Subwect + has/have + V3). • Identify the obwect: “several measures” is the obwect in the active sentence, and it becomes the subwect in passive. • Passive structure for Present Perfect: Obwect + has/have been + V3 + (by + subwect) + remaining words. • Apply the structure: “Several measures have been introduced (by them) to improve classroom participation.” The agent “by them” is optional because it is obvious and not essential here. Why other options are incorrect: • (b) uses Simple Past “were introduced,” which changes the tense and meaning. • (c) uses Present Continuous Passive “are being introduced,” which is a different tense/aspect. • (d) uses Simple Present Passive “are introduced,” again changing the tense. So the correct answer is (a)

questions.question: The following sentence has been split into four segments. Identify the segment that contains a grammatical error. If there is no error select option d as your answer Sitting in the dim library and tracing the faded map with a trembling finger, (A)/ the secret passage was discovered by the curious apprentice, (B)/ and the group burst into laughter when the “treasure” turned out to be old exam papers tied with ribbon. (C)/ No error (D)
questions.explanation: Option (B) contains an error. Explanation: The opening participial phrase “Sitting in the dim library and tracing…” must modify the doer of that action. But the subwect in (B) is “the secret passage,” which cannot logically sit or trace—this creates a dangling/misrelated participle. Corrected sentence: “Sitting in the dim library and tracing the faded map with a trembling finger, the curious apprentice discovered the secret passage …” Grammatical rule used: A participial phrase (V1+ing) must logically and directly modify the subwect of the clause that follows. Example: “Reading the diary carefully, the apprentice noticed a hidden clue.” Information Booster: Ask “Who is doing the -ing action?” That person/thing must be the subwect right after the comma.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank. They stood ______ the familiar people, wondering whom to talk to first.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (d). Correct preposition: “amidst” Explanation: • Amidst means “in the middle of” or “surrounded by,” and it is used to show that someone is among a group of people or things. • The sentence suggests they were standing among familiar people at a gathering and were deciding whom to speak to first. Hence, “stood amidst the familiar people” is correct. Hindi meaning: “amidst” = “के बीच में / के बीचों-बीच”. Example: He remained calm amidst the chaos. Why other options are incorrect: • Option A: across (Hindi: के पार/सामने) indicates opposite direction or crossing; it does not mean “among.” • Option B: behind (Hindi: पीछे) indicates position at the back; it does not fit the idea of being surrounded by people. • Option C: before (Hindi: सामने/पहले) indicates in front of or earlier in time, not “among.” So the correct answer is (d)

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option that can substitute the highlighted segment in the given sentence. I would gladly accompany you to Iceland if you had asked me.
options: I would have gladly accompanied you
I was to have gladly accompanied you
I will have gladly accompany you
questions.explanation: The correct substitution of the highlighted words is (a) I would have gladly accompanied you. Explanation: · The sentence talks about a past unreal condition – something that did not actually happen in the past. · The “if” part – “if you had asked me” – is in past perfect tense (had + V3), which shows a condition that did not occur. · In such Type 3 conditional sentences, the main clause must be in the form: · would have + V3 (past participle) · Therefore, the correct main clause is: “I would have gladly accompanied you…”, not “I would gladly accompany you…”. · This shows: I didn’t go with you because you didn’t ask me in the past, but I was ready and willing. Grammatical rule used (Third Conditional / Unreal Past Condition): · Structure: · If + had + V3, subwect + would have + V3 · Used to talk about imaginary or unreal situations in the past and their possible results. · Example structures: · If he had studied harder, he would have passed the exam. · If they had left earlier, they would have caught the train. Example (based on the given sentence): · I would have gladly accompanied you to Iceland if you had asked me. Why other options are incorrect: · (b) I will gladly accompany you – Incorrect · “Will gladly accompany” talks about the future, but the “if” clause “if you had asked me” clearly refers to a past unreal condition. The tenses do not match. · (c) I was to have gladly accompanied you – Incorrect · Awkward and unnatural structure here; “was to have accompanied” is rarely used and does not match the conditional form with “if you had asked me.” · (d) I will have gladly accompany you – Incorrect · Wrong combination of modal + verb form. After “will have”, we must use V3 (past participle), but here we have V1 “accompany”. It should be “accompanied” if this structure were to be correct, but even then the tense would not match the unreal past condition. Information booster (Conditionals): · Type 1 (Real Present/Future): · If it rains, I will stay home. · Type 2 (Unreal Present): · If I were rich, I would travel the world. · Type 3 (Unreal Past): · If you had called me, I would have helped you. – same pattern as the given sentence.

questions.question: Change the following from active to passive: Policy experts had repeatedly evaluated the framework’s assumptions since its release.
options: The framework’s assumptions had been repeatedly evaluated by policy experts since its release.
The assumptions were being evaluated since its release by experts.
The assumptions had evaluated by policy experts since its release.
The assumptions had been under evaluation by experts since its release.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (a). Explanation: The given sentence is in active voice and uses past perfect: “had evaluated”. In passive voice, the obwect becomes the subwect, and the structure becomes: Obwect + had been + V3 + by + Subwect. Structure: Active voice: Subwect + had + V3 + Obwect + (adverbs) + since + time point. Passive voice: Obwect + had been + V3 + (adverbs) + by + Subwect + since + time point. So, “Policy experts” (doer) becomes the agent with by, and “the framework’s assumptions” (obwect) becomes the new subwect: “The framework’s assumptions had been evaluated by policy experts ...”. The adverb “repeatedly” is correctly placed before V3 for emphasis. Why other options are incorrect: • (B) uses “were being evaluated” (past continuous passive), which changes the tense and is incorrect with “since”. • (C) is grammatically incorrect: “had evaluated” needs “been” and V3 form. • (D) is an awkward nominal phrase (“under evaluation”) and does not match the exact past perfect passive conversion. “So the correct answer is (a)”

questions.question: Sentences of a paragraph are given below in wumbled order. Arrange the sentences in the correct order to form a meaningful and coherent paragraph. A. A sound body and sound mind makes one happy and cheerful. B. People having good health are highly ambitious. C. A man becomes intelligent, powerful and genius. D. Health is the root of prosperity and success.
questions.explanation: Correct sequence: B C D A Step-by-step explanation of order: Why other options are incorrect: So the correct answer is (d) The correct option is (d). • Sentence B is a suitable opening because it introduces the general idea that good health influences a person’s attitude and goals: healthy people are ambitious. • Sentence C logically follows because it describes further positive outcomes of health on an individual (intelligent, powerful, genius). It continues the cause-effect development introduced in B. • Sentence D comes next as a broader concluding generalization: health leads to prosperity and success. It summarizes the benefits mentioned in B and C at a larger level. • Sentence A fits best as the closing statement, giving a well-known concluding idea: sound body and mind make one happy and cheerful. It provides a final, balanced closure. • Sequences starting with A do not introduce the broader theme as clearly as starting with the health-based ambition point (B) or root statement (D); A works better as a concluding remark. • Putting D too early without first illustrating individual outcomes (B, C) weakens the paragraph’s explanatory buildup.

questions.question: Sentences of a paragraph are given below in wumbled order. Select the option that arranges the sentences in the correct logical order to form a meaningful and coherent paragraph. A. Similarly, a person who has never lost has never found the woy of winning. B. It is better to wear out than to rest out. C. It is better to play to win than to lose. D. When one puts oneself on the track of excellence, it brings talents to light. E. It is better to aim for excellence and not get lost in success. F. It is said that those who have never angered anyone are a failure in life.
options: D, F, A, B, C, E
C, D, E, F, B, A
B, A, D, C, E, F
C, B, E, D, F, A
questions.explanation: The correct option is (d). The correct sequence is C, B, E, D, F, A. This order creates a coherent flow of ideas focused on striving, excellence, and learning through real-life experiences. • C introduces the central motivational idea: it is better to play with the aim of winning than to accept losing. • B strengthens the same theme by suggesting active effort: it is better to wear out through work and struggle than to become inactive. • E then refines the focus: one should aim for excellence rather than being distracted by mere success. • D logically explains the benefit of aiming for excellence: it brings hidden talent to light and develops ability. • F adds another life lesson: meaningful action often leads to disagreement; never having angered anyone may indicate a lack of impact. • A begins with “Similarly,” and therefore must come after a preceding life lesson; it concludes by stating that one who never loses cannot truly experience the woy of winning. Key linking clue: Sentence A starts with Similarly, so it must follow another general life observation (here, F), making F → A a strong concluding pair. So the correct answer is (d)

questions.question: Select the sentence containing the homonym of the highlighted word: The falconer released the hooded tiercel at dawn.
options: The wounded tiercel was returned to its perch.
The trainer praised the agility of the tiercel in flight.
The village paid a tiercel of its grain as a levy.
The tiercel soared swiftly above the cliffs.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (c). Meanings of the Word: TIERCEL: (1) A male falcon, typically smaller than the female. (2) (Rare/archaic) A third part; one-third of something. The highlighted word tiercel in the given sentence means a male falcon used in falconry. In archaic usage, tiercel can also mean a third portion. Option analysis: A, B and D use tiercel as a male falcon (same meaning as the original sentence). C uses tiercel to indicate a third share/portion, which is a different meaning, so it correctly uses the homonym. Example (homonym usage): Only a tiercel of the estate remained after the division.

questions.question: Fill in the blank with the most appropriate conwunction: You should submit the assignment today ____ she won’t be allowed to attend the final class.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (a). The sentence expresses a consequence if the first action is not done. The conwunction phrase or else is used to show an alternative negative result: “Do this, otherwise that bad result will happen.” (Hindi meaning: “नहीं तो / वरना”). So the correct sentence is: “You should submit the assignment today or else she won’t be allowed to attend the final class.” Example: Leave now or else you will miss the bus. Why other options are incorrect: • unless – means “except if” and usually introduces a condition in a different structure (Hindi: “जब तक नहीं”). Here, the meaning required is “otherwise,” not a conditional exception. • and – is an additive conwunction (Hindi: “और”), but the second clause is not an addition; it is a warning/result. • since – indicates reason/time (Hindi: “क्योंकि / तब से”), but the second clause is not a reason; it is a consequence. So the correct answer is (a)

questions.question: Select the word that is closest in meaning (SYNONYM) to the word given below. Epistle
questions.explanation: Correct answer: (c) Letter Explanation: The word “Epistle” means a letter, especially a formal or long letter (also used for letters in the Bible). • Hence, the closest synonym is Letter. Word Meaning (English): Epistle = a formal/long letter Hindi Meaning: Epistle = पत्र/चिट्ठी (विशेषकर औपचारिक पत्र) Example (Epistle): He wrote a long epistle explaining his decision. Meaning of the correct option: • Letter = written message Hindi Meaning: Letter = पत्र/चिट्ठी Example (Letter): I received a letter from my friend yesterday. Synonyms (Epistle): letter, message, missive, note (formal) Antonyms (Epistle): (no direct single-word antonym) but opposite idea: silence/no reply (contextual) Meanings of other options (why incorrect): • (a) Feeble = weak (कमज़ोर) → unrelated. • (b) Fable = moral story (नीतिकथा) → unrelated. • (d) Hymn = religious song (भजन) → unrelated.

questions.question: Select the MISSPELT word from the options.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (d). Step-by-step explanation: • We must select the misspelt word. • Option (D) Amuunition is misspelt. • The correct spelling is Ammunition, which means weapons such as bullets, shells, and missiles used in fighting or hunting. (Hindi meaning: गोला-बारूद) • Therefore, option (D) is the incorrect spelling. Example: The soldiers were supplied with enough ammunition for the mission. Meanings of all the given options: • Option (A) Appendix: An additional section at the end of a book or document; also a small tube-shaped organ in the body. (Hindi meaning: परिशिष्ट/अपेंडिक्स) • Option (B) Annexure: An attachment or supplementary document added to a report/contract. (Hindi meaning: संलग्नक) • Option (C) Annihilate: To destroy completely. (Hindi meaning: पूर्णतः नष्ट करना) • Option (D) Amuunition: Incorrect spelling; correct word is Ammunition. (Hindi meaning: गोला-बारूद) Part of speech note: Ammunition is generally used as a noun (uncountable noun in most contexts). So the correct answer is (d)

questions.question: Change the following from active to passive: The reviewers would have amended the draft if fresh evidence had been presented.
options: The draft would have been amended by the reviewers if fresh evidence had been presented.
The draft would amend by the reviewers if fresh evidence had been presented.
The draft could be amended by the reviewers if fresh evidence had been presented.
The draft was amended by the reviewers if fresh evidence had been presented.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (a). Explanation: The active sentence uses a conditional with “would have amended,” which is the perfect conditional (would have + V3). To convert to passive, the obwect “the draft” becomes the subwect, and the verb changes to “would have been amended” (would have been + V3). The agent “the reviewers” is added with “by”. The if-clause “if fresh evidence had been presented” remains unchanged because it already describes a past perfect condition. Structure: Active: Subwect + would have + V3 + Obwect Passive: Obwect + would have been + V3 + by + Subwect Correct passive sentence: “The draft would have been amended by the reviewers if fresh evidence had been presented.” Why other options are incorrect: · B: “would amend” is incorrect in form and missing “been”; also “amend” should be past participle in passive construction. · C: “could be amended” changes the modal meaning from “would have” (hypothetical past result) to “could” (possibility). · D: “was amended” changes the tense and meaning; it states an actual past action, not a hypothetical past result. Information booster: When you see “would have + V3” in active, the passive almost always becomes “would have been + V3”. Preserve the conditional nature of the sentence. So the correct answer is (a)

questions.question: Select the option that can be used as a one-word substitute for the given group of words. A belief that God is present in Nature or a belief that regards the universe as a manifestation of God
questions.explanation: The correct option is (a) Pantheism. Pantheism is the belief that God is present in nature and that the universe and God are essentially the same. It treats the entire universe as a manifestation of the divine (प्रकृति में ईश्वर की सर्वव्यापकता का सिद्धांत). Example: Pantheism teaches that divinity exists in every part of nature, including rivers, mountains, and living beings. Meanings of the other options: • B: Atheism means disbelief in the existence of God (नास्तिकता). • C: Feminism means the belief in and advocacy of equal rights for women (नारीवाद). • D: Cynicism means distrust of human sincerity and a belief that people act mainly for selfish reasons (निंदक दृष्टिकोण/संशयवाद). So the correct answer is (a)

questions.question: A sentence is provided in indirect speech. From the four given options, choose the one that most accurately conveys the sentence in its corresponding direct speech. The coordinator asked whether the files that were expected to be completed last month had been forwarded to the evaluation panel.
options: “Did you forward the files meant to be completed last month to the evaluation panel?” the coordinator asked.
“Had the files expect last month been forwarded to the evaluation panel?” the coordinator asked.
"Have the files that were expected to be completed last month been forwarded to the evaluation panel?" the coordinator asked.
“Were the files due last month forwarded to the evaluation team?” the coordinator asked.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (c). Explanation: The given sentence is in indirect speech and reports a yes/no question using “asked whether”. To convert it into direct speech, we must restore the interrogative form and keep the meaning intact. Key conversion rules used: • “asked whether/if” becomes a direct question with a question mark. • The reported verb phrase “had been forwarded” indicates an action completed before a past reference point. In direct speech, the speaker’s perspective can be represented using a natural present perfect passive question: “Have ... been forwarded?” • The descriptive clause “that were expected to be completed last month” must remain to preserve the exact detail. Option (C) correctly forms the question: “Have the files ... been forwarded ...?” and keeps all essential information, including “expected to be completed last month” and “evaluation panel.” Why other options are incorrect: • (A) changes the structure and focus by using “Did you forward” (simple past active) and slightly shifts the passive meaning. • (B) is grammatically incorrect (“expect” should be “expected”) and the sentence construction is faulty. • (D) changes “evaluation panel” to “evaluation team” and reduces accuracy; also the tense and phrasing alter the original meaning. “So the correct answer is (c)”
